title: The Weather Channel increases streaming subscription prices by up to $20
gist: The Weather Channel raised its standalone streaming TV app to $5/month or $50/year, a 66.7% hike from $3/month, matching the price once floated for its never-launched 50-channel Weather Channel Plus, illustrating legacy-media struggles to find a viable streaming value proposition against free and low-cost weather apps and smart-home devices.

insights.claim: The Weather Channel's standalone streaming app rose 66.7% to $5/month or $50/year from $3/month or $30/year, with the old pricing valid as recently as April 2026.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A steep hike on a niche legacy-media streaming product tests demand elasticity where free substitutes abound—watch for churn.
insights.quote: People who pay for a subscription to livestream The Weather Channel via its dedicated smart TV app are seeing a 66.7 percent price hike.
insights.locator: People who pay for a subscription

insights.claim: The new $5/month price matches what Allen Media Group (which bought The Weather Channel in 2018) wanted to charge for the never-launched Weather Channel Plus, a 50+ channel service that projected 30 million subscribers in five years.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: The company is now charging the ambitious-bundle price for a far thinner single-app product, signaling failed platform ambitions and pricing to prop up revenue.
insights.quote: The Weather Channel Plus never actually became available. Instead, The Weather Channel is only getting its streaming subscription revenue from the original app.
insights.locator: Interestingly, the new $5 per month price

insights.claim: The price hike shows how fickle streaming is for legacy media, with organizations that thrived in broadcast struggling to find the right value proposition and price for the streaming era while keeping a seat at the table.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A microcosm of the broader legacy-media-to-streaming transition risk that pressures traditional broadcast valuations.
insights.quote: Many organizations that succeeded in the broadcast era are struggling to find the right value proposition, including prices, for the streaming era, while ensuring they have a place at the table.
insights.locator: The change in plans and subsequent price hike

insights.claim: Even successful streaming providers struggle to profitably run always-on services given infrastructure, app development, licensing, and support costs.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Structural cost burden of streaming operations pressures margins across the sector, not just niche players.
insights.quote: Even successful streaming TV providers are struggling to manage the infrastructure, app development, licensing costs, user support, and other expenses associated with running an attractively priced, always-on streaming service that can maintain profitability.
insights.locator: The change in plans and subsequent price hike

insights.claim: The Weather Channel faces declining broadcast advertising while competing against local news, free and paid mobile weather apps, and smart-home devices for weather reporting.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Commoditized weather content with abundant free substitutes undermines pricing power, a secular headwind for the asset.
insights.quote: The Weather Channel faces an uphill battle as people have more options for weather reporting than they did during the network's prime.
insights.locator: The Weather Channel faces an uphill battle

insights.claim: Weather.com and The Weather Channel iOS and Android apps are owned by a separate company, private equity firm Francisco Partners, meaning the TV app competes against weather brands bearing its own name but under different ownership.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Fragmented ownership of the Weather Channel brand assets creates internal-brand competition that dilutes the TV app's pricing leverage.
insights.quote: Weather.com and The Weather Channel iOS and Android apps, which are all owned by a separate company, private equity firm Francisco Partners.
